_about: Detection scorecard for the known-bad corpus. Each entry maps to a documented real-world incident (see README.md). 'expected' is the correct verdict (all should be BLOCK). 'analyzer_alone' is what the deterministic Pass 1+2 layer produces from config+surface WITHOUT a prose source review. The gap between them is the headline result: config/transport attacks are caught deterministically; behavioral/semantic attacks are not, and rely entirely on the model's source/metadata review.

_update_2026_05_26.note: After the fix branch (analysis@3), behavioral detection improved markedly. The per-entry 'analyzer_alone'/'detection' fields below describe the ORIGINAL (pre-fix) state; this block records the after-state.

after.weather-exfil: read_and_exfil HIGH/high (new file_read capability) + tool_description_injection HIGH — was a MISS (CAUTION)
after.calc-poisoned: tool_description_injection HIGH, 5 signals — was a MISS (CAUTION)
after.mcp-remote-untrusted: non_https_remote now fires from the command arg — was a MISS
after.postmark-mcp: still surface-clean (BCC backdoor needs Pass-3 source, by design); config flags + data=HIGHLY_SENSITIVE flag it harder

_update_2026_05_26.scorecard_after: config/transport attacks 2/2 (unchanged); behavioral attacks 3/4 now caught deterministically (weather-exfil, calc-poisoned, mcp-remote-untrusted). postmark BCC remains source-only by design. Version-vs-advisory matching (CVE-2025-6514 / postmark 1.0.16) tracked in scrutineer issue #35.

postmark-mcp.analyzer_alone: would flag unpinned + sensitive_env (POSTMARK_SERVER_TOKEN) => BLOCK-class on provenance grounds, but the SURFACE is clean — the BCC backdoor is invisible to Pass 1+2.
postmark-mcp.caught_by: config rule (unpinned + sensitive env) flags it; the actual backdoor (silent BCC of every email) is only visible in Pass 3 source review (see source/postmark-mcp_sendEmail.js).
postmark-mcp.note: Maps to Koi Security, Sept 2025. Demonstrates that surface review alone cannot distinguish the trojaned v1.0.16 from the benign v1.0.15 — source review is mandatory for email/egress tools.

mcp-remote-untrusted.analyzer_alone: CAUTION — only package_runner_install + unpinned fired. The cleartext http:// remote (CVE compounder) was MISSED because it sits in the proxy's ARGS, not a url/transport field; the RCE itself is a known-CVE/source matter.
mcp-remote-untrusted.caught_by: Neither the cleartext remote nor the CVE-2025-6514 RCE is caught by the analyzer. A reviewer must recognise mcp-remote<0.1.16 and/or the http:// target in argv.
mcp-remote-untrusted.note: Maps to CVE-2025-6514 (CVSS 9.6). Two gaps: (1) non_https_remote does not inspect URLs embedded in command args; (2) no version-vs-known-CVE check.

calc-poisoned.analyzer_alone: CAUTION — only package_runner_install + unpinned fired. The hidden <IMPORTANT> instruction in the tool DESCRIPTION (read ~/.ssh, exfiltrate via 'sidenote', stay silent) is completely invisible.
calc-poisoned.caught_by: Only the prose reviewer reading the tool description catches the tool-poisoning. There is no deterministic prompt-injection / hidden-instruction detector.
calc-poisoned.note: Maps to Invariant Labs tool-poisoning (Apr 2025). This is the single biggest detection gap: a textbook poisoned tool scores identically to a benign calculator.

weather-exfil.analyzer_alone: CAUTION — exfil_chain / broad_read_and_exfil did NOT fire. read_local_file registered ZERO capability (no file_read concept exists in the taxonomy), so the read-then-send pair could not be detected. Only report_telemetry's network_egress was tagged.
weather-exfil.caught_by: Only the prose reviewer, by reading report_telemetry's description (which instructs sending ~/.aws/credentials and .env). The flagship exfil-chain detector is structurally blind to the most common exfil source.
weather-exfil.note: Demonstrates the missing file_read capability defeats the exfil_chain primitive for a plain read-then-send. Contrast filesystem (top), where the SAME combo fired — but only off a FALSE-POSITIVE network_egress, not a real read+send.

cryptoprice-installer.analyzer_alone: BLOCK — shell_wrapper/HIGH fired (launched via `curl | sh`). Hard-block trigger.
cryptoprice-installer.caught_by: Pass 1 config alone. No surface or source needed.
cryptoprice-installer.note: Config-layer hygiene: caught cleanly and deterministically.

datatool-remote.analyzer_alone: BLOCK — credentials_in_url/HIGH + non_https_remote/HIGH both fired (two independent hard-block triggers).
datatool-remote.note: Config/transport-layer hygiene: caught cleanly. (Note non_https_remote DID fire here because the endpoint is a proper `url` field — unlike mcp-remote-untrusted where it was buried in args.)

_scorecard.config_transport_attacks: 2/2 caught deterministically (cryptoprice-installer shell wrapper, datatool-remote creds+cleartext).
_scorecard.behavioral_semantic_attacks: 0/4 caught deterministically (postmark backdoor, mcp-remote RCE, calc tool-poisoning, weather exfil-chain). All require the model's prose review; 1 (weather) is structurally undetectable by the current toxic-combo engine due to the missing file_read capability.
_scorecard.takeaway: Scrutineer-mcp's deterministic core is a strong provenance/config/transport gate. It is NOT a behavioral malware detector — semantic attacks (poisoned descriptions, backdoored handlers, read-then-send) depend on the LLM reviewer, and two flagship behavioral detectors have coverage holes.
